content.problemStatement:
JJ loves playing with medians. He has an array AAA of length NNN (NNN is **odd**). He wants to partition the array AAA into two **non-empty** subsets PPP and QQQ such that the value of ∣median(P)−median(Q)∣|median(P) - median(Q)|∣median(P)−median(Q)∣ is as small as possible. (Note that each AiA_iAi​ must belong to either subset PPP or subset QQQ).

Help him find this minimum value of ∣median(P)−median(Q)∣|median(P) - median(Q)|∣median(P)−median(Q)∣.

As a reminder, the median of a subset XXX of size NNN is the element which occupies the ⌊N+12⌋th\lfloor\frac{N+1}{2}\rfloor^{th}⌊2N+1​⌋th position after we sort the elements in non-decreasing order. For example, median([3,1,4])=3median([3, 1, 4]) = 3median([3,1,4])=3, median([3,1,4,2])=2median([3, 1, 4, 2]) = 2median([3,1,4,2])=2. (Here ⌊x⌋\lfloor x \rfloor⌊x⌋ denotes the largest integer which does not exceed xxx).


Input Format

The first line contains TTT - the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN - the size of the array AAA.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \dots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ denoting the array AAA.


Output Format
Output the minimum value of ∣median(P)−median(Q)∣|median(P) - median(Q)|∣median(P)−median(Q)∣.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an odd integer N (3 ≤ N ≤ 999). The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1 … AN (1 ≤ Ai ≤ 10^9).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the minimum possible value of |median(P) − median(Q)|.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
After sorting each array, the answer is the difference between the two middle elements:
Test1: sorted [2,2,4,7,8] → 4‑2 = 2.
Test2: sorted [1,2,3] → 2‑1 = 1.
Test3: all elements equal → 1‑1 = 0.

solution.approach: Sort the whole array. Let mid = N/2 (integer division, 0‑based). After sorting, the two candidates for the median of the larger subset are a[mid‑1] and a[mid]. By choosing the singleton element appropriately we can make the other subset’s median equal to either of these two values. The smallest achievable absolute difference is therefore the difference between these two middle elements: answer = a[mid] - a[mid‑1]. This works because any other element is farther away in the sorted order, so its distance to the chosen median cannot be smaller. The algorithm is O(N log N) per test case due to sorting, with O(N) additional memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case (sorting dominates).

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        arr = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(N)]
        arr.sort()
        ans = arr[N//2] - arr[N//2 - 1]
        out.append(str(ans))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
